questions.question: Explain the difference between istream, ostream, and iostream classes.
keyPoints: istream is designed for input (reading) operations
ostream is designed for output (writing) operations
iostream inherits from both istream and ostream, enabling both read and write
Examples: cin is istream, cout is ostream, stringstream can be iostream

questions.question: What are stream manipulators? Give examples of any four manipulators with their purposes.
keyPoints: Manipulators are functions that modify stream behavior or formatting
endl - inserts newline and flushes buffer
setw(n) - sets minimum field width
setprecision(n) - sets decimal precision
hex/dec/oct - changes number base
left/right/internal - controls alignment
fixed/scientific - controls floating-point notation

questions.question: Write a C++ program to copy contents of one file to another using file streams.
keyPoints: Open source file with ifstream in read mode
Open destination file with ofstream in write mode
Read from source character by character or line by line
Write to destination until EOF reached
Close both files

questions.question: Explain the different file opening modes in C++. Distinguish between ios::app and ios::ate.
keyPoints: ios::out - open for writing (default for ofstream)
ios::in - open for reading (default for ifstream)
ios::app - append mode, all writes at end of file
ios::ate - at end, can write anywhere but initial position is end
ios::trunc - truncates file to zero length
ios::binary - binary mode without newline translation
Difference: ios::app always appends, ios::ate positions at end but allows writing elsewhere

questions.question: What is stringstream? Explain its usage with an example.
keyPoints: stringstream is a stream class that operates on strings
Enables treating strings as streams for parsing/converting
Can convert numbers to strings and vice versa
Useful for string formatting and tokenization
Example: stringstream ss; ss << 123; string s = ss.str(); converts int to string

questions.question: How do you handle errors while performing file operations in C++?
keyPoints: Check is_open() after opening file
Use fail(), bad(), eof(), good() functions to check stream state
fail() detects logical errors (type mismatch, file not found)
bad() indicates fatal errors
eof() indicates end of file reached
Clear error flags with clear() to recover from some errors

questions.question: Explain the difference between get() and getline() functions in C++ streams.
keyPoints: get() reads a single character including whitespace characters
getline() reads a line of characters up to a specified delimiter
get() returns the character as int/char, getline() returns stream reference
get() leaves newline in stream buffer, getline() extracts and discards delimiter
getline() stores result in string/char array, get() stores in char variable

questions.question: Explain the stream class hierarchy in C++. Draw and explain the relationships between various stream classes.
keyPoints: Diagram showing ios_base as base class
ios derives from ios_base adding formatting features
istream derives from ios for input operations
ostream derives from ios for output operations
iostream derives from both istream and ostream for bidirectional I/O
fstream derives from iostream for file I/O
ifstream derives from istream for file reading
ofstream derives from ostream for file writing
streambuf is separate hierarchy for buffer management
Key member functions and purposes of each class

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program to read student records (rollno, name, marks) from a file and display them. Also handle exceptions and errors appropriately.
keyPoints: Define Student class with rollno, name, marks
Open file in input mode with error checking
Read records using >> operator or getline()
Handle file not found, format errors using fail() check
Display records in formatted table
Close file properly
Use appropriate manipulators for formatting
Handle EOF properly

questions.question: Explain the concepts of stream buffers and formatting. Discuss various formatting flags and manipulators available in C++ streams.
keyPoints: Stream buffers (streambuf) handle actual data transfer
Buffers improve efficiency by reducing I/O operations
Formatting flags: setf(), unsetf(), flags()
Field width: setw(), width()
Precision: setprecision(), precision()
Fill character: setfill(), fill()
Base: hex, dec, oct
Alignment: left, right, internal
Floating-point: fixed, scientific, showpoint
Boolean: boolalpha
Examples and demonstrations of each

questions.question: Discuss random access in files using C++ streams. Explain seekg(), seekp(), tellg(), and tellp() functions with examples.
keyPoints: Random access allows moving to any position in file
seekg() positions the get pointer (read position)
seekp() positions the put pointer (write position)
tellg() returns current get pointer position
tellp() returns current put pointer position
ios::beg, ios::cur, ios::end define reference point
Example: file.seekg(0, ios::end) to go to end
Calculate file size: pos = file.tellg() after seek to end
Move relative: file.seekg(10, ios::cur)
